One example of this is in the salon. The doors open so much wider now, letting the fresh air in. Windows are so much bigger, allowing more light in. And the full height windows to the starboard side slide open to transform a functional side deck into a magnificent terrace on the sea. Well, you know, for the rest of this walkthrough part of the video, I think it would be great if you could just take a look at the yacht on your own without me following you around and talking to you all the time. Then when you're finished, let's meet back up here cuz I'm really interested in telling you about some of the features on this yacht that I believe make speaker stand out from the crowd. Heat. Heat. Heat. How was that? She really is a beautiful yacht, I think. And to be honest, a standard Ferreti 1000. Let's call it an ordinary Feti 1000 is a great yacht. But the owner of Spa did not want an ordinary yacht. They wanted something extraordinary and they were prepared to spend a considerable sum of money to get it about €1.8 million in fact. Now there are some big ticket items in with that price and there are many many many smaller items too. So let me take you through just some of them. And we can do no better than starting with the engines. The baseline offering for Ferreti for this model provides for MTU V16 engines with just over 2,100 horsepower. It will give you a 24 knot top speed. Or you can pay extra for a model of MTU V16 engines with 2,368 horsepower that will give you a top speed of 28 knots. No prizes for guessing which option speaker has. The generators were upgraded from 27 kW to 35 kW. And in fact, one generator can handle the load of the entire yacht. Speaker was fitted with a 40 horsepower hydraulic stern thruster for ease of maneuvering. An alpha laval fuel polishing unit installed as well as a Hammond Blackwater treatment system. The navigational instruments were upgraded. Much of the galley equipment upgraded and extra plug sockets were wired in throughout the yachts. As you'll know if you've ever searched around for somewhere to plug in your phone charger. This latter upgrade does improve the quality of life on board. As does this customordered parket flooring with a special anti-slip surface that I found actually very effective and not at all abrasive to the feet. Custom handrails were fitted to enter the cabin areas so that it's easy to descend even in rough weather and with a newborn in your arms. A biminy was added for the stern seating area and some of the televisions too were upgraded and a fuel pump was added to the tender garage so that the tender can be refueled on board. Even the tender itself is beautifully speced with its own bimin, an electric inflator and a freshwater shower. One of the most significant upgrades though is that she is commercially compliant with a multis flag. Now this required a whole series of changes and upgrades to the yachts. From the handrails to the firefighting system and from the fuel filters to the port holes. I think you'd agree this is not an ordinary Ferreti 1000. speaker is quite extraordinary. That latter upgrade, the commercial compliance was made because Spa is actually available on the charter market, which if you're thinking of buying her, could be rather handy because it means that you could charter her for a week or two to make sure that she really is the yacht that you and your family would like. and having spent a bit of time with the crew and experienced the chef's cooking as well, I can say that you would not regret chartering her for a couple of weeks. But actually, that's not the reason that I think that speaker stands out in that sub10 million euro 100 ft production yacht market. Ferretti say that when they released this model, it was the result of 40,000 hours of concept development, 4,250 hours of naval design, and 9,650 hours of prototyping the molds. Between you and me, when I worked at Custom Line and we released the new 97 to replace the old 94, we used the same molds. Okay, we tweaked them a little bit, but that is what production yacht manufacturers do when they can. They use the same molds. Not here. This was totally new molds, totally new engineering, a huge investment for Ferreti Group, and a totally new model of yacht. So when you have a yacht that is the result of that kind of effort and money put into its development and its design substantially better than the previous models and then it's purchased by an owner who puts that kind of effort and money into further improving it. That's that is why I say that speaker does stand out from the crowd. But there's one final thing that I want to say. I was originally taught not to take a full turn around the cleat then do my figure eights and finish with the locking turn. Always used that and never had any trouble with it. I found if I do a full turn around cleat before the figure 8 the lines tend to bind on them selves and make it hard to undo. But really, whatever works for you the way you diet is fine. 

I understand that if I were dealing with a boat considerably larger and heavier than mine 30 foot sailboat that a single figure 8 with a locking turn might cinch up and be hard to undo. One big variable that is rarely talked about is the size of the cleat relative to the line. That requires a little thought and an adaptation to be secure.
  4. @busterevetts1864 [2 likes]: Excellent video Jon.. You explain things so well.. Keep up the great work..
  5. @amcconnell6730 [1 likes]: It is worth noting that the US Coast Guard no longer locks cleat hitches for any reason because of an incident involving ice where they couldn’t get the line off the cleat that had a lock as part of the knot. Now they use many figure eight loops around both horns to tie off lines. The Washington ferries use the same method for their dock lines, and for working lines where getting the line off quickly is even more important, such as with a headsail sheet, never lock it.
  6. @anonymousperson2578 [1 likes]: Good job bloke. I neva considered using a single rope for dock line and spring line.
  7. @dalefountain9269 [1 likes]: Very helpfull!
  8. @rikstone7059 [1 likes]: Using both fixed loop ends at the centre cleat can avoid short ends.

  1. @woodycooper7551 [1 likes]: Umm OK, I dock my boat just like that when there is a crew on board.  I didn't see any special technique different than what I'd normally do.  That was indeed a very smooth docking and I'll go ahead and say you do that better than I do.

  4. @Mooieboom: Sorry, but this is not how you secure a line to a bollard. If you want to teach people how to do it, demonstrate it correctly.

Simply wrapping a few turns randomly around the bollard posts is careless and unsafe. In strong wind the boat could easily break loose because nothing is actually locking the line in place.

The correct method is to take your initial turns neatly around the bollard to absorb the load, and then finish by twisting the line so it forms a figure six or nine. The standing part of the line must pass underneath the final loop. That last crossed turn creates a locking hitch.

This way the loop clamps the line against itself, and the harder the boat pulls, the tighter it grips. Properly secured, the load increases the holding power instead of weakening it.

with the Tide while the dock stays fixed to the piling so you're gonna set up your fenders where the dock pilings end up hitting the boat and right underneath the rub rail or the widest part of the - Stories by Feadship - Bakira: Kind: captions Language: en Strange thing, it was cloudy that day. The wind whispered and the waves embraced us. They say Neptunes breath can be treacherous. So I let the current lead the way. And found an interesting view. I met this guy. Odd fellow. Thick accent but quite Renaissance. ‘’O hello sir, didn’t mean to scare you off’ he said. He smiled at me as the bartender who knew all my sins and secrets. So... I decided to linger for a while. Plunge in, give it some air. “The bar is closed but I’m having a drink...” “Can I also get you something perhaps...”? Before I could answer he served me a 7 and 7. He told me great stories. How he'd been casted for jobs all over the world Worked for luxury dames with lush gardens. Who he had to read bedtime stories to in the early mornings. Quoted John Milton flawlessly when he found out about my academic heritage. Did a great impression of De Niro. And showed me his scars from a fight in Kinshasha. And then...as easy as making the drink... he started to play this piano. Just like that. ‘’It’ s an old song...”. He said. “What’s your name...”. I asked ‘’Bakira...’’ He replied and smiled. guaranteed i mean it depends on every vessel but typically you are not guaranteed weekends specifically if the guests are on board if you have got a six-week charter you're going to be working every that kind of naughty stuff you've got all your toiletries included your laundry is taken care of you know every morning you'll put it in the laundry bag drop it off it'll come back nicely folded at of utilitarian they are quite compact and you have to be a very neat and tidy person to live on board so there you go that was a pretty quick guide to super yachts and everything that's involved you - How to Run a $14 Million Superyacht | WSJ Operating Costs: Kind: captions Language: en - [Presenter] This 128 foot long super yacht runs almost $14 million to purchase, but that's just the beginning of its expenses. - [James] It's a city, it's a resort, and it moves. - [Presenter] But imagine if that resort sat on top of one of the planet's saltiest, wettest environments. Yacht owners can also expect to pay 10 to 20% of the yachts value each year simply to maintain it. Superyachts or yachts longer than 82 feet can sell for hundreds of millions of dollars. This means maintenance could be tens of millions per year. Here's what goes into this superyacht's estimated annual budget of nearly 1.9 million. - [James] Welcome aboard Snowbird. - [Presenter] Cost that up even before owner set sail. - [James] Dockage fees are very expensive. They range from $3.50 a foot to 7.50 a foot per day. - [Presenter] Dockage fees can be so high for superyachts in part because there's a large demand for a limited number of spaces, but if owners keep their yachts docked, they could face other large maintenance expenses. - All the works of the bottom of the vessel gets scummed up with oysters and clams and barnacles. A lot of these electronics, they get fouled up if they sit dormant for a long time. It's definitely better to use it than let it sit. - [Presenter] While docked, the yacht can connect to the Marina's power grid for basic operations, but when it's out at sea, this 368 ton vessel requires fuel, a lot of it. - For someone like myself, I call this boat yoga. What we have here is the day tank. This holds approximately 800 gallons of fuel. See the red? That's the amount that's in the tank. - [Presenter] In total, this yacht can hold almost 12,000 gallons of fuel. Plus, other water vehicles like its jet skis and support boat will need even more gas. - Generally, we burn anywhere from 60 gallons an hour to 120 gallons an hour. - [Presenter] At around four and a half dollars a gallon. - [James] It costs approximately $360,000 per year to fuel Snowbird. - [Presenter] But day-to-day maintenance is actually the fourth largest operating cost and it can get messy. - Today we're gonna be changing out the crankcase ventilation filter. That keeps your turbos and your after cooler from getting gummed up, and it also helps reduce oil consumption and reduce air pollution by further combustion inside the engine. This part probably costs $60, $70 to replace, but it has immeasurable value in prolonging the longevity of your motors. Maintaining the engines on a yacht like Snowbird typically costs around $60,000 a year, and you're looking at parts, time, and the materials involved to change the oils and keep everything up and going. - [Presenter] That's just below deck. Superyacht owners also need to invest in keeping the rest of the yacht in pristine condition, like the teak flooring. Teak is typically used on yachts because it's a weather resistant luxury material, but it's also challenging to maintain. - [Josh] When we scrub the teak, oftentimes it only lasts roughly a week or two depending on the traffic on board. It wants to go back to its natural color, and, you know, between rain and rinsing it down, you kinda wash away all of that, you know, hard work that you've done. Annually the teak flooring costs about $18,000 to maintain. - [Presenter] Adding on smaller expenses, you're already looking at just over $1 million. But by far, there's one cost for maintaining superyachts that completely surpasses the others, the crew. The estimated budget for Snowbird's crew is over $800,000 per year and that's because super yachts must always have a crew even when docked. - You have to take care of the teak the paint, wash windows, machinery. Rust never sleeps, maintenance never stops. - [Presenter] Cost towards their health insurance, travel expenses, uniforms, and cell phone bills can add thousands to the budget. Adding in crew expenses brings the annual operating cost estimate to nearly 1.9 million a year, but that's only when there are no major repairs or maintenance while owners generally spend around 10% a year in maintenance costs for new superyachts. - [James] It's kind of a guideline, but as she gets three years or five years, or hits her 10 year, it's gonna be more than 10%. - [Presenter] Every five years or so, superyacht owners have to shell out for one of their largest expenses. - Paint jobs are sometimes from a hundred thousand to a million bucks, depending on your size of your boat. Paint is about $1,800 an inch, - [Presenter] Salt in the water and air corrodes the yachts paint. To repaint the entire yacht, it needs to be hauled out of the water, then it can take months to complete a fresh paint job. There are also other major potential expenses. Some yacht owners need to spend hundreds of thousands of dollars every few years on detailed inspections. And if a yacht fails in inspection, repairing or replacing it could run $200,000 or more. - Certain boats, you can't get the engines out. You have to cut a hole in the side and then reweld it, recertify the hole. Now you're into the multi-millions to change out an engine. - [Presenter] To recoup some of these costs, superyacht owners will rent out their vessels. The charter fee for this super yacht ranges from $125 to $145,000 per week, that covers the boat and the crew, but renters also need to pay for additional expenses like fuel, food, and customs fees. Plus, there's a customary tip of 18 to 25% for the crew members. At best, superyacht owners might break-even on maintenance costs with income earned from chartering. - I don't think it's a profitable situation. At best, they're covering their salaries and the boat's way as it travels. Wear and tear is always there and the money would have to go back into the vessel. If you didn't properly maintain your vessel, your value would decrease. Most people that buy yachts of this size and this stature really wanna take care of them. (upbeat music)

engine geared to a shaft turning a huge variable pitch propeller a variable pitch propeller increases efficiency by turning or feathering the propeller blades to maximize performance and minimize required power needs extra power comes from 3gen sets and megawatts of storage batteries and then as a new thing thruster an as amusing thruster is basically a steerable propeller it provides maximum thrust at low speeds and directs thrust by rotating the entire thruster units through an azimuth of 360 degrees whereas most thrusters are only used while maneuvering during entering or leaving a port Savannah uses her thruster whilst at sea in the slipstream of the variable pitch propeller Savannah is the first year to operate in this way the system reduces energy loss of the main propeller to increase efficiency and performance each Bank of the Corvis 1000 kilowatt hour batteries has a management program and the computer regulates which ones to charge and how much all of this gives Savannah a range of 6500 nautical miles using 30 percent less fuel compared to a similar sized yacht at 2,200 gross tons and it's interesting design look it kind of looks a bit like a Bonetti to me like you and your boat sitting in a harbor or near a very nice island and emitting either noise or exhaust gases or maybe an oil spill this impacts the natural world at that locality which means the flora the fauna or that little bit of nature which you call humanity so how do we look into organizing that impact that is done via a life cycle analysis and that is shown here on the right so a life cycle analysis takes into account all the different phases during the life of a product of the yacht so for us that means materials build use and disposal and those phases we express usually in eco points so eco points are a way to calculate all the kinds of impacts that we have it is for instance that global warming by co2 but it is also acidification it can be land resource depletion it can be human toxicity which is added up all together in eco points so how do we look at it from our in manufacture we see mining for the steel and aluminum of our boats we see the uh production of the - Princess Yachts | Sailing on the waves of silence | Simcenter #SimcenterMarine: Kind: captions Language: en When I first came to development, we used to develop everything just using manual skills, manual labor. We'd have a 2D drawing from our design team which was one designer at the time. Obviously that's moved on from there to now a very comprehensive, almost full 3D model that generates CAD data. From the time I started in development, it's massively changed, it's a 360-degree turnaround really. The tools we depend on most are Simcenter NX and obviously Teamcenter because of the version control etc. and we can see where we've come from and where we want to aim to if we generate views that craftsmen are capable of understanding, of seeing. So it's important to present that information to them in a way that they can have an effect, and they do. We're currently market leaders, and that's down to those guys having an input, down to our designers pushing the envelope, and it's a great place to be. The core advantages of Teamcenter and Simcenter NX, are that we can simulate and build the boat in 3D before we start, so we're making the mistakes in the virtual world, not in the real world and we're getting solutions to problems that have been longstanding in the marine industry. We typically start with looking at the performance of the boat, we try to forecast the NVH performance, then set up some targets and work towards those targets. In development, we look at a database of the metrics available to us, and we say yes this is the level of performance we want to have for a particular boat. Then we do a superextensive test period, every one of those boats stays in test for a minimum of 5 weeks, for the biggest boats it's a few months sometimes. Every Princess boat is comprehensively tested, and from my side, I use tools like Simcenter Testlab and Simcenter SCADAS hardware to get the most information available. Michal's been with us doing NVH for us for years now, and has taken us from nothing to quite an impressive range of data that we're gathering. We're using that throughout the range to understand how the structure affects the vibration, the noise that you hear, but NVH will set the benchmark for structure, will set the benchmark for our customer's experience on the boat, because if you're drinking a gin and tonic at 25 knots, if you can get the NVH right, get it to the correct standard, you're now enjoying your gin and tonic at a nice smooth level pace, without hearing the noise of diesel engines or structural airborne noise around you. In the past, we've relied on human nature, ears, eyes, to try and self-diagnose it. Sometimes, a hit-and-miss formula, changing parts when they don't need to be changed, the data that Michal collates can narrow that down to an engine part, or a shaft's part, or a part of the boat that we can nail down and change specifically, rather than scattergun an attempt. We've developed our version of the digital twin on our M Class yachts, which is going to get better as time goes on, and as we grow into the new technology, that will also backfill through the rest of the range, and of course, as we develop new products, it will become part of the process, and will happen in a natural way over a period of time.

- The Secret Man Who Stole Princess Diana’s Heart: Kind: captions Language: en Diana's real love wasn't Dodie. For 2 years, she had a secret relationship with a heart surgeon. The surgeon who had performed the operation was Haznat Khn. Haznat was 36 years old. A brilliant cardiothoracic surgeon originally from Pakistan but working in London. Diana's fame was the biggest obstacle to their relationship. Haznat told Diana that he couldn't live in the spotlight. He was a surgeon who needed to focus on his patients and his work. He wanted to preserve the privacy of what they had shared. Hasnot finally spoke. In 2006, nearly 10 years after Diana's death, Hasnot Khan gave a rare interview. - Sea Ray Trailering Tips  Tire Pressure and Weight: Kind: captions Language: en C asked Facebook fans to submit questions about trailering then took those questions to the experts at GMC I'm Robert Krauss from GMC truck I'm the trailering engineer there and I've been working with hitches and trailer ratings for about 20 years I have a continuing problem blowing tires on trips longer than 200 miles what do you recommend with regards to maximum tire pressure it's unusual to hear about Tire blowouts today today's tires last a lot longer than they did uh 20 30 years ago for instance and blowouts are really uncommon if there's an issue it's usually going to be due to road hazards or to improper loading you always want to have your tire loaded to the number that's on the door or door frame label that number been set based on a number of items such as fuel economy handling and load carrying capability so you really don't want to play with that uh the pressure you want to stay with the pressure at the rating that's on the door label to ensure your trailer and boat meet the vehicle's tow capacity is there a rule of thumb to apply when calculating gross weight to determine the the trailering capability of the vehicle the best thing is to follow the manual um we've got the ratings published right in the owner's manual specific by the model and engine now the way we publish ratings is based on an unloaded vehicle and and the trailer weight exactly as it's listed if if you put extra gear or supplies in the trailer or people in the tow vehicle you've got to account for that and make sure that stays under the gross combination weight rating and really the only way to know for sure is to take the tow vehicle and trailer load it up and ready for the trip and take it to a scale and actually weigh it C would like to thank our Facebook fans for submitting their insightful trailering questions

- Hatteras Hull and Construction (2018) Factory Video - By BoatTEST.com: Kind: captions Language: en the following opinions are solely those of boat Escom and its test captain hi cam Steve for boat test comment today we're inside the Hatteras yachts facility specifically we're gonna be taking a look at the lamination process of their larger yachts one of the distinguishing features about a Hatteras yacht is areas that you can't see as the owner and that is the hull so we're gonna take a closer look at some of the things they're doing Hatteras utilizes the resonant fused process not only for the manufacturing of its hulls but also for its stringers and bulkheads as well this process has two distinct advantages first it's more of a rigid structure that holds its shape and arduous conditions it's in second it enables a glass to resin ratio of 60 to 40 which saves weight and maximizes strength the interior of the hull the main structure is made of this de vente cell foam core the coring is scored to better allow it to conform to the shape of the mold and as you can see it's flexible and what it allows it to do is when they go through the resin infusion process it gets the resin down in there and this is what makes that whole one unitized piece now it comes cut out in specific shapes for a particular model and each one has a number in that way the craftsmen know where to place it within the hull after applying the gel coat a skim coat and the outer laminate the de venise el foam is placed in the hull above the waterline the complete whole is vacuum bagged and the resin infusion begins in this cutaway cross section of the hull and you can see the outer and inner layers of glass the foam core and then all voids are filled with resin the resin is not only fills the voids between the coring but the coring itself becomes completely encapsulated by the resin after the hull has been resident fused hatteras actually sandblast the entire interior and you get this sort of rough finish what that enables them to do is when they put the resin in fused bulkhead in here and glass it in it gives them a more perfect bond here you can see the bag being placed over the stringers which have been manufactured outside of the hole at the bottom of the boat they will be resonant fused in a vacuum to the hull the same process is done with all bulkheads which are created with the same resin infusion method outside of the hull now I'm inside a GT 63 hull and here you can see one of the resonant fused bulkheads we talked about but it has been last in and this is essentially one unitized hold piece now and I can tell you well it's hard to demonstrate on video it's like a giant chunk of granite it's so solid as you can see there are four huge longitudinal stringers stretching from bow to stern they transfer the stiffening of the bulkheads from an egg crate shape structure into the bottom which gives its hull immense strength an interesting design feature about the GT series is that the fuel tank is completely integrated into the hull between the stringers by using the fiberglass hull bottom stringers bulkheads and flooring for the sides of the fuel tank Hedera saves weight builds even more strength into the keel and it also lowers the boat center of gravity making it more comfortable in a Seaway how Duras bulkheads are thick for sound deadening and strength but they're also light our FP pipe is cast into the bulkheads and stringers to prevent chafing of wire looms of plumbing and these are just a few of the construction details that had a Rashad uses to build what are among the strongest yachts in the world for boat Escom I'm Captain Steve we'll see you on the water [Applause]

- Common Boat Types Explained: Kind: captions Language: en can you sleep on your fishing boat and fish off your surfboat yes should you no stick around to find out all the great things about the different types of boats hi i'm sean from lenscove lessons and boating one thing is 100 true and that is that you can pretty much do almost every different type of boating activity on any type of boat so sometimes we see people literally fishing off of cruisers or sleeping in pontoon boats and it's totally possible but if you're in the boat market and you're new to boating you should really think about what your primary activity is and find the right boat for that once you've owned one or more boats and had boating experiences for years this part of the process becomes much easier so even though there is a ton of information we are going to talk about the best features of each of the 15 types of different these are the most popular boats on the market and we're going to add a very high level talk about why you might want to choose one particular boat over another we've got tons of videos on lens cove lessons and boating so if you enjoy this make sure you throttle that like button and jump on board by subscribing for more in the future on today's video we're going to talk about aluminum fishing boats bass boats bay or flat boats center consoles ski and fish bow riders deck boats high performance boats pontoons water sports or wake surf boats cuddy cabins mid cabin cruisers salon cruisers fly bridges and trawlers aluminum fishing boats are like the minivans of fishing boats they do it all you've got different styles which either have a side console or a dual console with a windshield and they tend to have higher sides and are what we would classify as multi-species fishing boats they're great for getting out on the water and just tossing a line enjoying the day with friends and family or really just escaping from reality bass boats tend to be fiberglass they're lower to the water and are designed primarily for bass fishing because they are easier to bend over to pick out by hand a larger smallmouth bass they're typically high horsepower fast boats to get in tournaments or a day out on the water to the fishing hole quickly and they are really driven for one or two people to be out on the water fishing for bass all day long a bay and a flats boat are most popular in salt water environments they are technically a center console but they have lower sides for getting into shallow water in the inner coastal bay areas and in the flats in some of the marshy areas and because of that they're not great in rough water the big brother to bay flats boats are a center console and they are typically more offshore worthy they are fishing boats that can be used as day boats but they have higher freeboard and are more comfortable when you get out into big water if you're in large lakes or out on the ocean fish and ski boat is the transition between a fishing boat and a day boat where you can do water sports they can be either aluminum or fab glass and they will have sections for relaxing like a typical bow rider they'll have other components such as trolling motors live wells and fish finders which make the ability to switch between family time and water sports and fishing more easy bow riders are probably the most popular choice for families getting out on the water when you want to just be able to do it all whether you want to throw a line for fishing ski or tube or you want to cruise along around the lake the bow rider gives you more rough water handling capability and can be purchased from anywhere from 17 feet up to 40 feet a deck boat is a larger wider version of a bow rider which has more space on board and because of that is slightly heavier so the performance characteristics aren't quite the same as a bow rider but gives you that open usable feeling which is a little bit easier to get on and off of than a bow rider a high performance boat typically is designed to have fun go fast and be seen they're great in they're meant to go from point a to point b be loud be fast be seen and look great a water sports boat or what we would call a wake surf wake board or water ski boat is purpose built to take your friends or family out on the water for a day of water sports whether you love to wake surf love to wakeboard or you just want to pull the kids all day long with a tube there is no better boat than a wake surf or water sport boat to take care of that because it is typically an inboard or v-drive power pontoon boats are like living rooms on the water they can take a ton of people with you they can store all kinds of gear for your day on the water they're great for transporting things back and forth to water-based cottages and truly are a great way to spend a day in relaxing and comfort as we transition to overnighting on a boat the first stop is with a cuddy cabin not many of these boats are made new anymore but you still find them on the used boat market and they are a great combination between an open con cockpit day boat and a cuddy cabin with a bed and possibly a porta potty or toilet so that you can stay overnight on it from there we graduate up to a mid cabin cruiser which is typically somewhere between 24 and 40 feet in length and has the ability to sleep two four or six people and really is like a cottage on the water but it still has the performance characteristics of being able to get up on plane and enjoy the day getting from point a to point b and really cruising inland or ocean waterways up one step from that is a salon style boat which is tries to be more on one level has more open concept main living room and galley area which is all at the waterline or above with a lower level of sleeping quarters next up is a fly bridge and a fly bridge is where you have the steering and control capability up on a flying bridge above the main cockpit or cabin and it's great for seeing around you and having good visibility and control with large areas for entertaining or living on board and this is a great offshore or big water boat to get out with particularly a couple or maybe even two big grand daddy of them all is a trawler or in some cases a yacht and a trawler is typically a displacement hull cruiser which is meant for slower travel more leisurely pace but more comfort stability and control particularly in large water and heavy seas as you can see there are a ton of choices when it comes to picking out what the right boat is for your family this is a high level overview and it really is just designed for you to have a basic knowledge of all the different boat types check out the description below for playlists of different specific boat types like pontoons runabouts bow riders deck boats cruisers center consoles and also in the description below you'll find a link to a one-on-one meeting with our team if you want to have a phone consultation so - 5 Reasons NOT to buy a live aboard sailing boat  - (Watch before you buy!): Kind: captions Language: en after three years living on a sailing boat full time sharing the very best bits of our adventures with you all in this video we want to try and balance out some of our overly romanticized depictions of life living on a sailing vote and share with you our five reasons not to buy a lever board sailing boat reason number one everything breaks out of all the reasons not to buy a liveaboard sailboat everything breaking was the reason we were the most unprepared for we're stuck for the foreseeable even if you put your boat on the heart and fix absolutely everything that's broken it won't be long before something has broken again if you want to live on a boat full time then you're gonna have to embrace this reality and spend your time learning to become a mechanic a plumber an electrician and a general shipwriter and everything else in between there's so much to learn and that's just the boat itself we haven't even touched on the skills you need to take her off and navigate to new and potentially dangerous places [Music] reason number two money boats consume an ungodly amount of money even if you do learn to do most of the work yourself the reality is that much of the equipment you'll need is expensive from broken hatches to replacing life rafts new engine parts replacing sails new rigging lift outs it all adds up and fast we're constantly hearing stories of people giving up life on the water because they were just totally unprepared for how much ended up costing the good news is that life on a boat can work out much cheaper if you're prepared to live a bit more frugally the way that we manage to make a small amount of money last us is by spending half a year living out at anchor always trying to scout out somewhere new to drop our hook and stay for free it's not always possible to stay out of expensive marinas but the more we can avoid them the longer we can make our money last if you're lucky enough to be able to work remotely from your laptop and the liverboard life could be a great fit for you but if you're more like us and very hands-on workers then at some point you're likely going to need to head back to shore and find some work for us we choose to tie up for the winter months and find jobs where we rebuild the sailing kitty and perform all the repairs and upgrades to the boat ahead of setting sail again as the warmer months arrive now some of you may be wondering hey but you guys are youtubers doesn't that give you an income too well yes we are and yes it does but for 99 of video makers out there the money you make from youtube is enough to maybe keep some beers in the fridge and of course hank's treat jar nicely topped up we are also very lucky that our humble little channel has the support of an incredibly generous group of patrons and this additional income helps repair and replace our constantly failing filming equipment which doesn't always play so well with the harsh salt water environment that comes with living on a sailing boat it also helps pay for the odd luxury stop in an expensive marina from time to time we're forever grateful to our patrons for all they do it's lovely to think that others are enjoying these adventures enough to support their future production reason number three the trade-offs giving up some of those land creature comforts in almost all cases moving on to a sailing boat is going to involve some downsizing serious downsizing and this is especially true if you're looking to move into an affordable monohull boat depending on how you intend to use your boat it could mean giving up mod cons like a freezer washing machine dryer hot water big tv reliable internet connection space for your many clothes and toys however with some careful planning the addition of solar panels some lithium batteries and a decent inverter and you can actually still enjoy many of the everyday creature comforts to be found on land even if you're living at anchor hot water but beware as lovely as life on a sailing boat can be with these amazing upgrades know that they don't come cheap if you'd like to install enough lithium solar a decent inverter with say a water maker too this is the kind of upgrade package which can easily set you back five figures even if you're doing the work yourself reason number four being well rested if you've decided to embrace the more frugal existence and avoid expensive marinas by living on your anchor then be prepared to never sleep quite as deeply and peacefully as you once did on land this reality hits you the hardest when you first start sleeping at anchor your biggest fear being that you haven't set your anchor properly and it drags while you're asleep or worse yet when you're not even on your boat at all there are things you can do to mitigate anchoring catastrophes and give you back a little peace of mind check over all of your ground tackle each year as part of your annual maintenance buy a modern oversized anchor that's easy to set set up multiple anchor alarms which alert you the moment your boat drifts outside a predetermined area and if you really want to be thorough you can even dive in and make sure your anchor is set correctly even if you take every precaution possible the chances are you will still sleep with at least a little part of your brain staying awake and alert listening out for new sounds or sensations in the boat's motion truth be told it gets better as time goes on and you grow more comfortable with anchoring but at first it certainly takes a little getting used to but don't let us totally put you off the idea of living on your anchor because what you may give up slightly in terms of rem sleep you more than make up for when you wake up in some of the most tranquil beautiful breathtaking locations which very often you have all to yourself reason number five your pets may hate it while it's true that most people don't take their family pet sailing it also seems to be the case that a high number of people who live on a sailboat full time do so with their little furry companions we got really lucky with hank he gets on great aboard cadoa he has no trouble getting around the boat he's very relaxed when we're on the move and often just found asleep well that is until we're joined by some of the amazing wildlife to be found with a life on the water in which case he's fascinated by all the weird and wonderful creatures he gets to meet one of the great pleasures we have is being able to take hank to so many more varied walks and he'd likely have ever ventured with in a more conventional life he's a happy dog whose life is rich with adventure and variety but of course there is a catch several in fact firstly despite our best efforts hank refuses to go to the toilet on board the boat so we have to carefully plan our voyages to ensure we can get him to shore at least twice a day normally first thing in the morning and again in the evening hank gets on fire when we're underway but we are super selective about when we move our home it's safe to say that as such we're very much fair weather sailors because even with hank's fantastic temperament on board anything too boisterous just it just wouldn't be fair on him so it seems as if we've worked a life on the water around our specific dog's needs but there's still a major catch especially when you choose to live at anchor because come wind rain or shine we need to get hank to show no matter what and that often means doing so in conditions that you'd much rather not venture out in is that something you could imagine doing for 10 plus years if not then that's a fifth good reason not to buy a live aboard sailing boat i hope this video has provided some balance to the lifestyle that's otherwise all too easy to romanticize thanks for watching see you next time

TRIONA SORENSEN: I think they were very astute people, that they could see quite quickly again through this maritime network that they had that there was a much wider world out there. And I think it's probably one of the most important aspects of the Viking age, that they were very much a people who looked outwards. They are exceptionally curious people and exceptionally brave in terms of where they want to go with their ships and why. NARRATOR: The Vikings had dreams of exploration and conquest. They needed a vessel to match. The long ship. Remarkably, some still survive. This is a so-called Oseberg ship, uncovered from a burial mound south of Oslo in Norway in 1904. It's now in the capital's Viking ship museum. It is the oldest Norwegian Viking ship. 95% of its original timbers survive. ELLEN MARIE NAESS: This Viking ship was preserved like a tinned Viking ship, really. No one had seen anything like it. It was even so well preserved that one day, the archeologist found a bucket, like a huge bucket, and in the bucket were apples, and they were still red. That's amazing. NARRATOR: The features that made it such an effective combat ship are also intact. Most important is its shape. The long ship, a catch-all term for all large Viking vessels, has a shallow draft, meaning that very little of the hull was below the waterline. This helped them move swiftly and in shallow water, deep into enemy territory. TRIONA SORENSEN: In the beginning of the Viking era, it's really the speed at which they were able to attack, that their ships just gave them mobility that was unheard of essentially, that they can just round a headland in a fleet of ships and sail right up onto the beach. GARETH WILLIAMS: They can also go right up rivers and sail deep inland. That meant that they could attack targets inland in Britain and Ireland. In France, they sailed all the way up to Paris. Same in Russia, again, they're penetrating deep into the river systems. NARRATOR: Soon, they ventured even further, to the Mediterranean and the Middle East and west across the North Atlantic. Their raiding ships had to withstand longer voyages and combat at sea. From the 8th century, they were equipped with a large sail, which was supported by a massive block of timber called the keelson. It spread the weight of the mast and the strain of the sail went underway. When there was no wind, the crew would place oars through holes in the upper strakes or planks. Parts of the decking were kept loose so weapons and plunder could be stored underneath. And it was designed to strike terror upon arrival. I think it probably would have been an incredibly awe-inspiring and terrifying sight, that we know from the historical depictions that these ships were highly decorated. Quite a few of them would've been painted. And I think it probably would've just struck absolute terror into the hearts of people if you knew that when you saw a fleet of 5, 10, 15 ships coming around the headland, and you knew that they were full of warriors and that they were sailing directly for your little coastal settlement, I think you would pack your bags and head for the hills as quickly as you could.

- A Sunken Treasure: The Warship Vasa: Kind: captions Language: en In 1628, The Swedish warship Vasa, technologically one of the most powerful vessels of her time, tragically sank on her maiden voyage. as the best preserved 17th century shipwreck in the world, The Vasa is not just a ship, she's a time capsule. Studying her has yielded invaluable insights into shipbuilding techniques, naval warfare and daily life in the 17th century. The reasons behind the Vasa's unexpected sinking are surrounded by speculation and common misconception. This is her fascinating story. What you see behind me is a unique survival from the early 17th century. This is a warship over 50m long, built in 1626 - 1627. It sank during its maiden voyage, but then was raised nearly intact nearly 333 years later. We're looking at a little bit more than 98% original material from almost 400 years ago. In the first part of the 17th century, Sweden was a geographically large but underpopulated country. It included most of what is now Sweden. All of what's now Finland, parts of the Baltic countries, and even a little bit of Russia. The King of Sweden in the early part of the 17th century, especially Gustaf II Adolf, believed that Sweden ought to be a major player in the power politics. They also saw that they believed that Sweden was surrounded by its enemies. Gustav Adolf, as part of this vision, reorganized the state to try to focus the resources that existed within Sweden onto the sole task of building a competitive military. First starting with the army and then eventually the Navy. And, in the case of the Navy, new kinds of ships. The King, however, believed that the future of naval warfare was going to be artillery duels between heavily armed gun platforms. And so, in his rebuilding of the Navy in the 1620s, he started ordering a new kind of ship to be built. The first of those ships to be built was Vasa. Vasa's keel was laid in February or March of 1626, and took about a year and a half to build. But waited a year to get its armament on board. Vasa was built using a method of shipbuilding that had been around since at least Roman times. And this was a method of designing and building ships that did not require any paper. There were no drawings, and this worked very well for the shipbuilders. but it had the problem that it was very difficult to administer. After many delays trying to complete the armament of the ship it finally set sail on the afternoon of the 10th of August, 1628. The ship only sailed about 1200 meters from the city, heading east down the harbor. And as it got to a gap in the southern Highland, the wind gets funneled out into the harbor at that spot, and it filled the sails and healed the ship so far over that the lower gun ports were in the water until it filled and sank. Most of the people who are on board survived. The ship was only about 120m from shore. If you could swim, you just swam ashore. If you couldn't swim, the upper parts of the masts were still out of the water, so you could cling on to something until a boat picked you up. When the ship was excavated, there were the skeletons of 15 people found inside the ship. There were a lot of questions immediately about what had happened. There was a lot of finger pointing of course. Nobody wanted to take the blame. But it emerged that they had a pretty good understanding of what had happened and why the ship had sunk. The first and most obvious is the ship is a bad ship. It's a bad design to start out with, and there's way too much ship above the waterline compared to the amount of ship carrying that weight below the water. However, that just means the ship was unstable. If it had not gone to sea it wouldn't have sunk.Somebody ordered it to sea about a month before the ship sailed. The captain who was supervising construction dragged the vice admiral in charge of procurement, and had him watch while he ordered 30 sailors to run back and forth across the upper deck to show the admiral that the ship wasn't stable. The admiral ordered that demonstration stopped because he was afraid the ship would roll over right there at the pier, and he appointed the captain who had demonstrated that the ship was unstable to be the ship's captain, and he sailed it very, very carefully. But he made one key error, because he had to show off the guns and fire salute. There was a way for water to come into the ship. The king probably has a little bit of responsibility. He was the one who created that decision making environment. The admiral didn't want to disappoint. He didn't want to tell them that the ship was unstable and couldn't get to its station. Nowadays, it's a popular belief that the reason the ship is unstable is because the king meddled in the construction. He ordered them to add another gun deck, or he ordered them to make the ship longer. But in fact, there's absolutely no evidence that backs that up. After Vasa sank, it wasn't a foregone conclusion that it was a total loss. People raised sunken ships. There were well known techniques for doing that. And when the ship landed on the bottom, it landed heald to Fort. So the first task was to get the ship upright again, and Bulmer succeeded in doing that almost immediately. And then he set about trying to raise the ship. Unfortunately, it had been embedded in the mud on the bottom that the force needed to lift It was much more than the weight of the ship. There were attempts to raise the ship all through the fall of 1628, the spring and summer of 1629, without success, before people really gave up. It was the site of a lot of activity over the next 35 years or so, trying to salvage originally the ship and then eventually recovering most of the guns. But after about the 1680s, people seemed to have mostly left the site alone. It wasn't until the 1950s that someone came along who had a vision of what Vasa could become. That it wasn't just a matter of salvaging the guns, or, as people did in the 1920s, blowing up old shipwrecks to salvage the timber to use to make furniture. But in the 1950s, a civilian engineer who worked for the Navy named Anders Franzén became interested in the history of the Navy, and one of the wrecks that he was looking for was Vasa. And what's interesting is for the first two years of his search, he didn't find it because he was looking in the wrong place. He was looking in the right stretch of the harbor, but on the wrong side. Partly because he, it's ironic to say, he made the mistake of talking to a professor of history to find out where the ship had sunk As he said himself: For those first two years, what he mostly found were stolen bicycles, abandoned shopping carts and dead cats. In the summer of 1956. He started looking in the right place. There had been a plan to build a bridge over the harbor, and so there was a big project of sounding the harbor bottom that revealed there was a big lump in the bottom, about 50m long and ten meters wide. And he was told: oh, that's just blasting rubble. And in fact, when Vasa was found, the whole upper deck was covered with blasting rubble. But Per Edvin Fälting said: no, I don't think that's blasting rubble. And so Franzén and Fälting together began dragging the harbor bottom. And on the 25th of August 1956, their drag encountered a big object. Frantzén put a coring device that he had developed that took a sample of it, and discovered it was black oak. So to make sure it wasn't just one timber they moved over about 20m and got another plug of black oak. So now that indicated there was a big old waterlogged wooden object lying on the bottom. A few weeks later a diving vessel visited the site. They went down to the bottom. Fälting walked towards where the obstruction was until he ran into a vertical wooden wall. And there's a square opening and he moved along and: oh, here's another square opening. And then he started climbing up the side on the on the planks that stuck out and said, oh, there's a second row of square openings. So that was when they knew they had found Vasa. Once the wreck had been relocated, the big question is, what do we do now? The vision that Franzén had was: let's recover the entire ship and make it the centerpiece of a museum that would tell the story of Sweden in its great power period. So the very first phase was an investigation of how much ship is there and what kind of condition is it in? And is it possible or even practical? And the method they chose was exactly the same method that Ian Bulmer had attempted in the fall of 1628. And they succeeded in a series of 18 lifts, moved the ship into shallower water. And they spent a year and a half there, closing in all of the openings in the hull. They rebuilt the collapsed stern, put covers over all the gun ports. And then in April of 1961, they winched the vessel to the surface. And once the upper edge of the hull was clear of the water all the way around. They put three gigantic pumps into the ship and pumped the water up until the ship floated again. And so Vasa, after 333 years, became the oldest ship ever successfully refloated, and it floated on its own keel so it could be placed on a concrete pontoon. And in fact, the ship still stands on that pontoon and we're inside it right now. The ceiling of this room is the deck of the pontoon, and this was the concrete box that was used to move Vasa around after it was lifted. Once the ship was raised, the next question was how can it be preserved? Normally, you would submerge an object in a tank of this material and gradually increase the concentration so that it would saturate the object. But Vasa was too big to fit in a tank. And so instead they decided to spray the ship. They also wanted the ship to be on display to the public because they needed some income. So Vasa became from November of the year in which it was raised, a tourist attraction, You would go into the hall where it was being preserved. The spray would be turned off while people could look at the ship from a viewing gallery. Then they would leave and the spray would be turned back on again. And then there was the discussion of how long do we have to spray it for? And there really wasn't any agreement about how long is long enough, because no one had ever done this before. And so in 1979, the spraying stopped and that was followed by a ten year process of controlled drying. Nowadays, we tend to say we probably would have liked them to spray the ship for another ten years. The conservation is never finished, but the what ship needed 60 years ago when it was raised was one thing. But it needs constant attention to maintain it. one day, far in the future Vasa will be nothing but dust. We cannot prevent that process. Our task is to slow that process as much as possible. But the timescale we're thinking in is centuries. And so major things that we've addressed in the last 20 years have included establishing a stable climate around the ship. The next project that we're working on now is the overall structural stability of the hull. Those heavily built decks that are the reason the ship sank are also a threat to the ship's long term survival. So we have to develop a support structure that carries the deck weight effectively and preserves the shape and structure of the ship. Vasa is not just a display case, it's a very active and dynamic research environment. A ship doesn't just represent the maritime part of society. It can tell us about forestry. It can tell us about the environment. It can tell us about the climate of that period. And so we have a research program that is very wide ranging, that tries to look at Vasa in the broadest possible context, to let it tell us everything. It can tell us about the people of Sweden and northern Europe 400 years ago. A current research project we're doing is looking at all the clothing on board the ship. These boxes on the table here are, fragments of cloth that were found on this ship. This one should be a good example. That's the back of a jacket. There's enough of that left there for us to tell you how tall that guy was. But some of that research is very detailed, focused. Looking at this cloth. We're doing that under the microscope. Some of it's on a much larger scale. Looking in archives for information about where they purchase things. And sometimes those projects are experimental. We want to understand how things were done. One of the most entertaining of those projects that we've done, was to try to understand how you would use Vasa as a warship. How did they think they were going to use force? Which ultimately leads to the question of why did they build that ship? Why did they build Vasa instead of a different kind of ship? Why did they choose that armament? What's the capability of officer in the tactical and strategic environment of the period? The story of the Vasa is about more than just a warship. It is a story of the people who built her, the era she was born into, and the enduring legacy she leaves behind, and perhaps most importantly, the challenges of preserving her for future generations.

- The Most Sustainable Boats | Hydrogen-Powered Ships, Solar Yachts & Renewable Energy Vessels: Kind: captions Language: en this video was sponsored by the nick mourne foundation for thousands of years man has relied on sailing the oceans for exploration fishing leisure and trade today the ocean continues to serve many of these purposes but often at the expense of marine biodiversity and overall environmental health ships contribute to of all air pollution but what if there was a way to reduce or even eliminate these emissions by using new technologies in this video we are going to be taking a look at five new innovative sea vessels which are helping stop environmental degradation by using renewable technologies such as wind solar and hydrogen let us know in the comments your favorite example at the end of the video this 100 foot catamaran known as the energy observer will be the first yacht to cruise around the world using hydrogen extracted on board from the sea water below it is a converted ocean racing catamaran that runs purely on the collaboration of a hydrogen fuel cell and solar power the catamaran is designed by sea lighten to prove that a yacht could run without the help of any fossil fuels during the day 200 square meters of solar panels charge up the boat's lithium-ion batteries any extra energy is then stored as hydrogen thanks to a special fuel cell each day the energy observer produces enough energy to power nine homes it has a central energy management system that coordinates energy production and transmission from its solar panels wind turbines and hydrogen fuel cell the goal is to be able to run these systems autonomously at different times so the boat performs at its peak efficiency [Music] every minute 17 tons of plastic waste is dumped into the world's oceans now imagine if this waste could be collected and used to fuel efforts that prevent environmental pollution the manta a first of its kind waste processing ship developed over four years by the sea cleaners has the ability to collect sort treat and repurpose or recycle up to three tons of waste per hour or ten thousand tons of floating waste per year like a monterey that survives by filtering seawater the manta is powered by transforming plastic waste into energy by also carrying onboard two smaller vessels cold modulus the manta seeks to intercept waste in shallows or narrow areas as well as the mouths and estuaries of some of the world's most polluted rivers collection carousels first collect the surface waste and transport it to a waste management plant on board operators then manually sort the waste by material separating plastic from glass and metal to be stored and brought back to land for recycling and organic matter like seaweed can be returned to the water plastic is then shredded and sent to the waste to energy conversion unit that melts the plastic and turns it into synthetic gas to produce electricity solar panels wind turbines and hydrogenerators work alongside the synthetic gas to power all of the vessels equipment as well as the spaces dedicated to scientific research education and awareness training for the public and prevention and corrective action against future waste the first missions of the manta are to take place in southeast asia if you've ever ordered anything from another part of the world chances are that that good was transported by boats with 80 percent of world trade traveling by sea how can shipping companies work towards incorporating more sustainable practices into the supply chain denmark-based mask the world's largest shipping company has decided to tackle this problem head on and in 2018 vowed to accelerate the transition to carbon neutral shipping its new targets include launching its first carbon neutral vessel by 2023 with the goal of a 60 emission reduction by 2030. its ultimate target however is to have its entire fleet of over 700 ships be carbon neutral by 2050 one of the most popular holiday options around the world cruise ships offer experiences with something for everyone to enjoy but these immersive experiences come at a big cost in 2017 the 47 cruise ships owned by the world's largest cruise ship operator emitted 10 times more sulfur dioxide emissions than every car in europe combined to combat this one of the largest cruise ship builders in the world french shipyard chantier de l'atlantic is currently developing cruise ships with 80 meter eco-friendly sales that will reduce cruise emissions by up to 50 percent known as the solid sail or ale drive the sails are made of fiberglass and carbon and are constructed in an accordion-like shape providing both flexibility and durability the sails would also be used in tandem with engines and propellers so although the solution does not entirely eliminate the harmful emissions it's the first step in the right direction for such a polluting industry mega yachts have always been status symbols of the ultra wealthy but despite the aesthetic glamour of these vessels the environmental impact and cost of running and stuffing such a large vessel is drastic silent yachts in partnership with yachting design firm 2 design have devoted years to the development of the latest and now one of the most sought after revolutions in yachting the silent 80 an entirely solar-powered luxury yacht available in a variety of models and sizes with electric motors small enough to fit under floorboards silent yachts not only boast minimal emissions and vibrating as opposed to noisy engines but it also offers more living space than competing yachts in an industry where owners spare no expense for amenities like headroom and living space silentyops offers more for the money and customization available at every turn this niche market is rapidly growing as well as pioneering other technologies using renewable energy including rechargeable battery powered engines hybrid propulsion and hydrogen fuel cells let us know in the comments your favorite example from this video and hit the like button if you enjoyed this one thank you for watching and we'll see you in the next one

  12. @petec9686: Lately I have been watching "Chasing Lattitudes" channel. 
If you are not familiar with it,  it is a very well done, informative channel, but, I think it is best described as the anti-CQ channel.

The owner of the channel is very practical. He has a formula for buying budget sailboats. This formula is no more than 20 years old and NEVER, EVER buy a boat with big overhangs.

His rationale is that you pay for total length, but you use waterline length. He says this is dumb and perhaps he is right. It makes no economic sense to pay for 40 ft but only use 28 ft. 

As I watched this review of this prime example of boat porn, I said to myself, "self, that dude is right. This thing is teeny-tiny inside. It is a terrible use of space."

But then I said to that practical side of me, "who cares?", this thing is drop dead gorgeous and if you do decide you want to wander the southern ocean, would you rather do it in a fast, light, spacious Oceanis where I suspect you'd feel like you were inside a dryer or this gorgeous, cozy, heavy, rock solid masterpiece? 

Pretty sure I would be happier bringing along less stuff and being comfy. 

Not saying that that dude is wrong. He is right about everything he says and you'll be happier anchored in a cove or in a marina in the Bahamas, but if bluewater sailing is your thing and you don't need to bring along lots of stuff, there is nothing better than the B40.

If they see high temperature, they turn off. If they see low water flow, they turn off. So, the good news when you neglect a generator is you rarely damage it. And that's quite often related to the exhaust system or the impeller itself. So I would say the the main thing you're looking for in a generator, of course, is checking oil and coolant, but you're also looking for water leaks cuz a small water leak of salt water left unchecked over time is what destroys a generator. And then the transmission um levels are going to vary and you need to read the manual on the transmission. A lot of these transmissions, the transmission is supposed to be on and idle. Um it's still okay to check it now when it's full just to make sure there's fluid on the stick. Um but read the manual on your transmission about where you're supposed to check the level. The reason a lot of them um want you checking at home when it's warm and they're running is there's a pump, a circulation pump and a cooler. And when the fluid is going around that system, um it's taking oil out of that reservoir and putting it in that system. When you turn off the engine and it's been off all night, that system may drain down depending on how cool it's now and the fuel level will change. So, read the manual on your transmission about how you're supposed to check it. Um, the transmissions are often overlooked. I go to survey all the time with 40-year-old oil in the transmission because it doesn't look dirty. Um, it doesn't have to look dirty. There's no combustion there, so it doesn't turn city. But change your transmission oil at the normal interval. Um, a lot of people just change it once a year when they do the engine, and it's probably more often than you need to do it, but it's way better than having an oil filter on and on survey day, a six-year-old. Um, so oil, transmission, and then coolant. These engines have a coolant reservoir very much like the car. And when they're empty, you don't know how empty it is. when they're empty, you don't know if the fluid's right here or if this engine needs another gallon to get it to come to here. So, try to keep it never below the low mark so you're not uncertain about how much coolant is in the engine. Once it drops below this and you don't know, you need to open the radiator cap. As soon as I do this, I'm going to introduce air into the system. Okay? But I'm going to open this. And a lot of times this engine, I can look right down at it. Um, sometimes the engine's up high and I'll just stick my finger in it and feel where the top of the fluid is. Okay. Um, if you know there's fluid in here and you ran the boat yesterday, there's no reason to open this cap. The only reason to open the radiator cap is because you don't know how far down you are or if you need to add fluid. If you overfill it, it just ends up in the doch. So, there's a lot of fluid in here, tens of gallons, and if you do the full fill way up here, it'll come out this cap and be all over the place. So, don't overfill it. It's fine here. It doesn't have to be right here on this line. We try to keep it in between when it's cold. This fluid expands when it gets hot. And this little scum line you see here, that is actually where when this engine is hot, this fluid comes all the way up to here because the fluid has expanded inside your engine. Um, the other thing we need to do next is look at how our fuel filters are set up. Pretty much any high-end boat you're going to have is going to have two primary filters for the engines. There's a lot of debate about what those should be and how it should be selected. I prefer it like this where I'm pointing to one filter or the other, not both. There's a setting where it'll run through both filters. What I like about having it on one filter and knowing the other filter is okay is if something was weird with my fuel and I really fogged this up really quickly, I could run down here real fast. I could pull this pin and I could flip to my other filter and keep on running. If I have it on both filters, whatever the problem was affected both filters and maybe it happened slowly over time. They're both clogged now dead in the water until I change these filters. So, I always like to know I'm running on one that's happy and I have another one that's clean. There's a vacuum gauge on these. I know you can't see it in this video, but there's a vacuum gauge that indicates how hard the engine is having to suck the fuel through. Um, I'll do another video on the details of that, but that's you can only look at that while it's running. But one of the indications you have for the state of these filters is this gauge. And we'll go into that in a more detail. Um this here on the uh engine, this is another fuel filter and this is a very fine filter and this is a primer and this basically pushes manually pushes fuel through the system. So when you change these filters, you're going to introduce air into the system and this little thing now I'm not going to do it now. You pump, you unlock it and you pump it and it forces the fuel up through the system and then back to the tank and gets the air out of the system. Okay? So, anytime you change one of these filters, you're going to have to go through this. These filters are rated in microns. So, most people run say a 30 micron filter here. And 30 microns, just imagine 30 parts per million or whatever. and the fine one on the engine might be five or two depending on the manufacturer. So, it's better to have a coarse one and a fine one than to put a real fine one. If you put a real fine filter first, it can clog up much faster and much more easily. So, if there's a 30 micron here and this is a 5 micron, this is only catching things between five and 30. If you put a 5 micron filter in here, a 2 micron filter in here, it's trying to catch with every little piece of every little speck microscopic speck dust. And then this isn't doing any of the work. So, generally, I like to run a fairly poor steel through 20 or 30 here. And then the engine manufacturer just dictates what this is. Don't forget this one. I had engines not running right on survey day and snow and changes and it was full and the fuel pressure was low and it didn't make RPM. So, don't forget about this stuff. Um, you don't have to do it as often as this, but every year when you're doing an oil change and stuff like that, make sure this gets changed as well. I find some people, you're in Timbuktu and you're just trying to get done what you can get done. You don't have a lot of parts, so you just do the oil change and main oil filter cuz you didn't have this filter. Um, have spare parts on board. even you're never going to do the work. Uh I recommend you have all the filters for everything on the boat. Two sets all the time. Then when you call a technician to come to the boat and you're doing work on the boat, you never have an excuse of I got to go back to the shop. I can't do it today cuz I don't have an car. That loop is a freshwater loop with glycol in it. And there's a circulation pump and that's part of the noise you hear in the back there. So right now there's two pumps running. And that freshwater loop is an endless circle of water going around the boat to all the parts of the boat. And you can see this owner's put a piece of tape here and they like to keep it right in there. And you see it's fluctuating a little bit. So, you just keep a visual eye on this system. So, one of the biggest safety items on a motor yacht is the exhaust system. The exhaust coming out of the engine is really hot and there's a lot of it's big engines, a lot of horsepower. And that exhaust is being cooled by seawater after it goes through the coolers of the engines and the transmissions and all that stuff. And that heat exchanger is subject to,000° air on one side and 130° sea water on the other. So there's a lot going on there. Okay. Um, pretty much any boat will have some sort of digital gauge system. They all fail. They have electronic senders that fail. They're not very granular. In other words, they don't necessarily show the levels real accurately. They give you a general idea. So, there's some valves here on this site tube that have to be used properly or you don't get an accurate reading. So, if I had 1,000 gallons of fuel in here and I broke it right here, I would have 1,000 gallons of fuel in the middle. So, there's a valve at the bottom and a valve at the top. Watch the fuel level here. And I've got, you know, a little over 500 gallons in here. Don't forget to open those valves. Had a couple guys run out of fuel and they look at this says 520 gallons. Well, if I ran all day and I didn't open those valves, it would still say 500 gallons. Really easy mistake to make and a very frequent beginner mistake to make. Uh, don't forget to close this valve.
